questions.text: The main purpose of laws like the 'Minimum Wages Act' is to protect:
questions.explanation: Minimum wage laws ensure that workers are not exploited by powerful employers.

questions.text: Who is responsible for making sure that laws are followed and enforced?
questions.explanation: Enforcement is critical; without it, laws exist only on paper.

questions.text: A 'Minimum Wage' is a wage that:
options: Can be anything the employer wants
Cannot be below a certain amount specified by the law
questions.explanation: It is a legal floor for wages to ensure basic survival for workers.

questions.text: In which city did the world's worst industrial tragedy occur in 1984?
questions.explanation: The Bhopal Gas Tragedy occurred at the Union Carbide pesticide plant.

questions.text: Which poisonous gas leaked from the Union Carbide plant in Bhopal?
questions.explanation: MIC is an extremely toxic gas that killed thousands within hours.

questions.text: Why did Union Carbide set up its plant in India instead of the USA?
options: Cheaper labor and lower safety standards
questions.explanation: Multi-national companies often seek lower costs and lax regulations in developing countries.

questions.text: The Bhopal tragedy was not an 'accident' because ________.
options: Safety procedures were deliberately ignored to save costs
The gas was meant to leak
questions.explanation: The company had cut down on safety equipment and personnel to increase profit.

questions.text: What is the role of the government as a 'Law Enforcer' in safety matters?
options: To help companies hide their mistakes
To inspect plants and ensure safety laws are followed
questions.explanation: The government failed in Bhopal by allowing the plant to operate in a crowded area with poor safety.

questions.text: Right to Life (Article 21) includes the right to a ________ environment.
questions.explanation: Courts have expanded the meaning of Right to Life to include environmental protection.

questions.text: What is the problem with 'Cheap Labor'?
options: It often involves exploitation and poor working conditions
It creates more jobs for everyone
questions.explanation: When wages are very low, workers often can't afford a decent life and safety is neglected.

questions.text: The government has passed laws to prohibit the employment of children under ________ years of age.
questions.explanation: Article 24 of the Constitution prohibits child labor in hazardous industries.

questions.text: Environment was previously treated as a ________ resource that anyone could pollute.
questions.explanation: Until recently, there were few laws to hold industries accountable for polluting air and water.

questions.text: Who should be held responsible for the environmental damage caused by a factory?
questions.explanation: The 'Polluter Pays Principle' states that the party responsible for pollution should bear the costs.

questions.text: Which court case established that the 'Right to Life' includes the right to enjoyment of pollution-free water and air?
options: Subhash Kumar vs State of Bihar
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court gave this landmark judgment in 1991.

questions.text: What is the goal of 'Social Justice' in the market?
options: To ensure fair transactions and protect the vulnerable from exploitation
questions.explanation: Laws act as a check on the market to ensure producers, consumers, and workers are treated fairly.

questions.text: CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) was introduced in Delhi transport to ________.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court ordered all public transport in Delhi to switch to CNG to clean the air.

questions.text: Many industries shift to ________ because of loose environmental and safety laws.
questions.explanation: This is often called 'dumping' of hazardous industries in poor nations.

questions.text: Workers often don't complain about poor conditions because ________.
options: They are afraid of losing their jobs
questions.explanation: In a country with high unemployment, workers have weak bargaining power.

questions.text: What is the 'Polluter Pays' principle?
options: Polluters get paid by the government
Polluters must pay for the damage they cause to the environment
People must pay polluters to stop
questions.explanation: It aims to make industries internalise the cost of environmental protection.

questions.text: Consumer protection laws ensure that ________.
options: Consumers get quality goods and are not cheated
questions.explanation: Laws ensure that products (like medicines or food) are safe and meet standards.

questions.text: Investment in safety measures is often seen as a ________ by companies.
questions.explanation: To maximize profit, companies often cut corners on safety equipment.

questions.text: The Bhopal gas victims are still fighting for ________.
questions.explanation: Decades later, the site remains contaminated and many victims have not received full justice.

questions.text: Why do safety laws need to be updated regularly?
options: To address new technologies and risks
Because the old ones are lost
questions.explanation: Laws must keep pace with industrial and scientific developments.

questions.text: A 'Vested Interest' means:
options: A personal stake in something, especially one with financial gain
questions.explanation: Companies have a vested interest in keeping costs low, even if it compromises safety.

questions.text: Which of the following is a way for the government to protect the environment?
options: Ordering factories to move away from residential areas
questions.explanation: The state uses its regulatory power to manage environmental health.

questions.text: Does the law allow the employment of children in 'Dhabas' or as domestic help?
options: No, it is banned under Child Labour laws
Only if they are paid well
questions.explanation: The Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act has been expanded to cover almost all forms of work for children.

questions.text: What is the meaning of 'Exploitation'?
options: Taking unfair advantage of someone for one's own benefit
questions.explanation: Exploitation happens when workers have no choice but to work in dangerous or underpaid conditions.

questions.text: Law and Social Justice means that the law should be used to ________.
options: Protect the weak and ensure fairness for all
questions.explanation: The ultimate aim of law in a democracy is to achieve a just society.

questions.text: The Supreme Court in India has increasingly focused on ________ since the 1990s.
questions.explanation: Environmental litigation has grown, often through PILs.

questions.text: When a factory pollutes a river, who is affected?
options: The people who depend on the river for drinking and farming
questions.explanation: Pollution is a negative externality that affects the whole community.

questions.text: Is the government responsible if safety laws are not enforced?
options: No, it's the company's fault only
Yes, it is the government's duty to monitor and enforce laws
Only if there is a protest
questions.explanation: The government acts as a regulator and is accountable for failures in enforcement.

questions.text: Which fundamental right prohibits 'begar' (forced labor)?
questions.explanation: Article 23 protects people from human trafficking and forced labor.

questions.text: Hazardous industries should ideally be located:
questions.explanation: This reduces the risk to human life in case of an accident.

questions.text: The word 'Liability' means:
questions.explanation: Union Carbide had a legal liability to compensate the victims of the gas leak.

questions.text: What is the problem with shifting polluting factories to rural areas?
options: It makes the city clean but pollutes the rural environment
questions.explanation: It simply moves the problem rather than solving it.

questions.text: Public awareness about ________ has increased since the Bhopal tragedy.
questions.explanation: The tragedy changed the way people and courts view corporate responsibility.

questions.text: Minimum wages are reviewed and revised ________.
options: Every year or periodically by the government
Only when the workers go on strike
questions.explanation: Revisions are necessary to account for inflation and cost of living.

questions.text: A 'Producer' in the market is one who ________.
questions.explanation: Producers include farmers, factory owners, etc.

questions.text: The government can also protect people by ________ prices of essential goods like kerosene.
questions.explanation: Price controls ensure basic items are affordable for the poor.

questions.text: Sustainable Development means development that meets the needs of the present without ________.
options: Compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
questions.explanation: It balances economic growth with environmental protection.
